Math.title: Calculus and Mathematical Analysis
concepts: Limits & Continuity: Evaluates the behavior of functions as they approach infinite or discrete boundaries.
Derivatives: Measures rate of change, instantaneous slopes, and optimization constraints.
Integrals: Calculates area under curves, continuous accumulations, and fundamental theorems.
lectures: Lecture 1: Limits of indeterminate forms and L'Hopital's Rule.
Lecture 2: Product, quotient, and chain rules for transcendental derivatives.
Lecture 3: Riemann sums and definite integration.

Computer Science.title: Data Structures and Algorithms
concepts: Time Complexity (Big O): Classifies algorithms according to their worst-case performance scaling.
Graph Traversal: Breadth-First Search (BFS) and Depth-First Search (DFS) topological orderings.
Dynamic Programming: Solves complex problems by breaking them into overlapping memoized subproblems.
lectures: Lecture 1: Asymptotic bounds, memory layouts, and array versus list overhead.
Lecture 2: Tree balance constraints, AVL rotations, and red-black tree conditions.
Lecture 3: Memoization arrays versus bottom-up tabular programming.

Biology.title: Molecular Biology and Genetics
concepts: DNA Replication: Semi-conservative replication overseen by DNA polymerases, helicase, and ligase enzymes.
Transcription: Synthesis of single-stranded messenger RNA from specific genomic templates.
Translation: Ribosomal decoding of codons into complete polypeptide amino acid chains.
lectures: Lecture 1: DNA fork mechanics, Okazaki fragments, and leading versus lagging replication.
Lecture 2: RNA Polymerase bindings, promoter regions, and intron splicing introns.
Lecture 3: Transfer RNA (tRNA) anticodon pairings and translation initiation complexes.

Chemistry.title: General and Organic Chemistry
concepts: Chemical Equilibrium: Reversible reactions governed by Le Chatelier's principle and equilibrium constants (Kc).
Thermodynamics: Enthalpy (dH), Entropy (dS), and Gibbs Free Energy (dG) determining spontaneity.
Electronegativity: Atomic pull on shared electrons determining polar covalent versus ionic bindings.
lectures: Lecture 1: Dynamic equilibria, reaction quotient calculations, and pressure perturbations.
Lecture 2: Hess's Law, calorimetry bounds, and state function calculations.
Lecture 3: Dipole moments, resonance structures, and steric hindrance.
